words: Your generation. Oh, no. No, no. Of course, in my generation, you could go farming. You stood a chance. Everybody trusted. In other words, you could get the long terms, and it was no problem to... Especially in my time where there was horses, that was still simpler, because the investment was virtually nil. You could go to a sale and buy the best plow in the gone country for a two -bottom plow, a 16 -inch two -bottom plow, or a six -horse gang, you call them. You could buy that for $25. Now you go out and you buy a tractor for $50 ,000, you buy a $10 ,000 plow, and you've got $100 ,000 in an outfit to farm, not only $300 ,000, but say $500 ,000 in it, and it don't meet. It's out of proportion. And that's the difference between now and then it was 20 years ago. And that seems to be getting worse. There's something about wealth that I want to ask you, and it has to do with status. And it seems like these days there's a lot of status attached to having money, to being wealthy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering, in the earlier years, did that mean, did that count for as much as it could count for a great deal? Did people who were wealthy see themselves better?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yeah, they were recognized. In other words, now, in Laidau County, for instance, I would judge that we have between two, this is an awful spread, but I'd say between two and five hundred millionaires. That is a little bit unbelievable, but it is on paper that's possible. Now, 50 years ago, at the time that I was beginning, a young man, a man that was worth $100 ,000, he was really considered a wealthy man. And yet today, they measured his success by the dollar. Yes, if he had $100 ,000, he was considered a very successful person. But that was history, and 9 chances out of 10, he was.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: If you had an estate, in those days, at $50 ,000, which would be the average farmer, why that was considered very, very good.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Often, this particular, these individuals, that was worth $100 ,000 to $200 ,000, they were loaning money to neighbors. They were helping finance the land purchase of somebody else or buying themselves. And of course, it was very, very easy. There was no income tax problem. So it was very easy to start. Once you had $100 ,000, it was very easy to get to the $200 ,000, it was far more easy to get to the $200 ,000 than it was the first $100 ,000, just the same way today. If you're young and you've got a good thing going, and you've got a million dollars, it's not too hard to become the second million. And that was true in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think that these men with wealth, were they deferred to by other people, by local people? Did it really count?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Did people...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Condemn them?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No. Was they deferred to mean kind of... Oh, I don't think so because nobody bragged about it if you were wealthy. In other words, that was a public opinion. And I had an uncle that was wealthy, as we know him to be wealthy. He was very conservative. He was frugal. He stayed home a lot. He was considered tight, but you didn't condemn him, or people didn't rush to him. No. He had a family, and I think his family was far more attractive than he and his money. I think that's what made life kind of interesting for him. In those days, there were large families, and his family had four girls and three boys, and he was seven. And so they made his life for him, but he continued, as long as he lived, to accumulate wealth. Because he was a farmer, and his boys... That's another thing that helped make your wealth, is your boys helped you on the farms.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: And it seemed to be all the old -timers, as a rule, even down in my area where I was born and raised, they were all good. They had a stable background. They weren't accumulating a lot of wealth because as their families became older, and the boys grew up, it was harder to save the money. In other words, the boys, the generation, they wanted a car, or they wanted better horses, they wanted better farm equipment. And where Dad had been getting along, the old -fashioned way, the old walking plows, and the two horse gang, well, that took his capital. And of course, that changed the picture, where it was harder to make that wealth grow faster. But it was still there. If you owned your land, I can remember, as I became part of the banking business, of knowing farmers who had a $2 ,500 mortgage on their farms, when I came to Troy, and this was also true in Kendrick, that $2 ,500 mortgage had already been there on their farm ten years. It had never been reduced. The interest was paid annually, and that continued to go year after year without a reduction. Now, that was because they had so many places for their money that they were getting the living they were making, that all they figured they had to do was pay their rent on the mortgage, and that set them up for another year. Well, as I continued to be in the banking business, I tried and I successfully managed to get farmers, and those kind of situations, to pay at least $100 a year on that principle. And just like the fellow says, he paid the rent for so long that he don't think he owes the principle, and it had become a picture that nobody could ever bother me for that principle. And yet he would never miss $100 to put on that principle ten years, yet it had paid for. And it worked, and it worked. And that's the principle of the Federal Land Bank, and all mortgage companies today. You don't get along without amortization, but that didn't be. That wasn't that way when I first went into the banking business. When you talk about wealth and the people who had had money, that didn't mean that they were singled out in any way as honored, particularly honored by the community. I don't think the word Sam is honored, as I think it was respected. If you had wealth, you were a little bit, as a fellow said, probably a little bit better than anybody else. That was an attitude to me. As young as I was, it didn't make any difference. But the poor guy, well, he didn't live, he could have lived better, you know, planning us. No, he's used to dirt, and yet they're real poor. You look down your nose to the degree that he wasn't trying to help himself. He might be the best friend in the world, he might be the best man. But the man that had wealth, and he had a decent car, and his house was well -built, and it was clean, and he had a good family, and he had the wealth. You couldn't help but put it all together and look up to him to the degree that, well, in my day, the president of the bank, of which I went to work for, was a farmer, retired farmer. The vice president was a farmer, he was my uncle, and so were the directors that made up the bank. They were all farmers, and they were successful, moneyed men to be in that position. They weren't considered any better than anybody else, but, oh, he's a banker and a farmer, well, he was just a little bit above. But you didn't single him out. There were so very few millionaires in Lado County during those years, and anybody that wasn't a millionaire, he was not a farmer. He had some other scheme or some other way of making it. Well, I've had the idea that on the other side, people were poor and remained pretty poor. A lot of them really had to struggle and didn't have the circumstances or whatever. Things didn't seem to break right for them, and they really were stuck. Of course, that's the individual. There are people that you could give $100 ,000 to, even farmers in those days. Let's just say $500 ,000, which was a fortune to them. Unbelievable. In five years, he would be broke. Now, that was history. You had to separate that from the good from the bad. He was a poor manager, he was a poor farmer. In the old adage, you heard him say, anything that man touched turns to gold. That's the successful financial operator who manages, who does things right, who is a good farmer, who takes care of his equipment. He's the man that everything he touches goes to gold. This other poor guy that struggles constantly, year after year, and he's never had anything, yes, he still is in the rut. We have those. We'll always have them. Right today, there are not very many in Lidar County, but there are some. Every community will have one or two or three or four. No matter what they do, they cannot do it right. That's the same thing in business, whether it be a farmer or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the average man, neither extreme? Were his prospects good?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: He never got to be wealthy, and he never was poor. He had a good living. He provided food for his family. He raised them, and he kept them clean. They were educated as near as he could, because those kind of young children in those days got jobs. They boarded, and they went to college for some way. They paid their way, and they got through. Yes, those persons you respected also. Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the question of cleanliness?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I've heard it said that before REA came in, before they had rural electrification in rural areas, that rural people felt inferior to the town and city people who were better dressed, cleaner.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there truth to that?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: You get into that position where you may take a bath only once a month, or whatever it happens to be.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: That farmer probably changes overall every two months. Those are the personal things. Yes, it is true. The advantage that you had in town, you had hot water, running water, lights, electricity, your neighbors dropped in on you constantly, people come to see you, there was a tendency to keep the house clean. They have the advantage. Out in the rural, once you got into that category, you were accepted those conditions. You were clean. Don't get me wrong. I'd say 80 % of the people were clean. But 20%, yes, they didn't know any different, and they didn't want to know any different, and that's the way they lived. And their children wouldn't be any better. But I think you can credit a lot of children for making their homes. For instance, if you lived in the country, they only took you to the 8th grade, so that child had to go to high school. He had to go to high school to the city. Pretty soon he was bringing his chum home to stay with him all night, or her all night. Maybe that child would say to mother, we've got to clean this up, we've got to live different. I was in Mary's house. Gee, they have it so nice in Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We can have that, can't we?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: And pretty soon you're teaching your mother, you're teaching your parent, not the old man. He probably stayed in the same rut, but the mother has pride. That happened many, many times. And that has been a very, very deciding factor to make people live better. And as a fellow said, there's no excuse for filth. None, period. Even the worst, hardest -of person in the world. There's water, it's cheap. You can be clean.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it would be the exposure of young people to the town?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, I think that was a very, very deciding factor in order to make parents keep up with the Joneses, in other words.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much difference between the town people and the rural people?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Very, very much so. When I came to Troy, the rural people would come into the bank, mothers. Very seldom did the wife transact banking business. Either the husband was too old or he wasn't well, and she would come in. This family would come in there and I couldn't talk to them because I could not talk to the Swedish or the Norwegian language. And they would, if I was alone, they would point or they would say cash or deposit. I could figure it out. But they couldn't talk it. And it was sad because that was how bad that home was. But that was not true right here in the town itself because the kids were coming home from school, high school. They were talking American language and the parents were understanding it and answering them in Sweden or Norwegian. So they were getting an advantage that the rural wasn't getting. Pretty soon, just over a period of year after year after year, pretty soon it got to be broken down. And yes, that was even common. Ollie Bowman's. I think that lots and lots of their conversations that went on in their home with Swedish. Because they're again, the kids, the kids, I think to this day they can, they're talking of the Swedish language is very limited. They can understand it. But they would answer in American and the father would talk to him in Sweden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take you very long to be accepted by the Swedish?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh my. Yes, it took me a long time to be accepted in Troy. I was here in 1926. I would say I was here a good 10 years and maybe longer and in the minds of a lot of them before I was accepted as a very, very close friend. They would treat you right. They would confide. But it wasn't the same because they couldn't talk the language. Swedish people are very clannish. That's always been a, so are German. So are the rest of them. They like to stick together. But I think in those days the Swedes were more so than ever where you had a Swedish and Norwegian combination. But oh no, you don't become a Swede overnight. I was put in my place many times that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Put in your place for being too friendly, too full?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No, no, you just were, I'm waiting, I wait till Mr. Paulson will wait on me. And even though I knew what they were doing, I could do it. But oh no, no, I'd rather have that old man wait on me because he's my speed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the Swedes and Norwegians in those days, did they have a keen rivalry between them or was it more of an actual union?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No, I'd say there was a keen feeling. It was a little bit under the boards, in other words under the table. If they got into difficulty they would stick together. But if you got two of them separated and they got into an argument, then they could express themselves. Yes, there was an undertone. I think the Norwegian felt that he was inferior to the Swedes. Because I think you noticed that most during the Second World War when Hitler invaded Norway and took it over, you know, there was a lot of Norwegians resented it because it was them to a Swede.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why should you be any different than us? Why should he conquer our country and not touch Sweden?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh, he's afraid of us. See, they rub it in and that happened, you know, and it hurt. Oh yes, that was true. And of course it was common knowledge. I heard it many, many times. You know what a Norwegian, ain't a goddamn Swede. You know what a, yeah, he's a Swede with his brains knocked out. See, that was common. And the kids, you know, they'd rub that in all the time. And I could never tell a Swede from Norwegian. They were all the same to me. But not them, they knew. But this town was really just about completely Swedish. Oh, well, or in the Anno region.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, it was probably in Troy, right?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I would say that it probably was a little bit more Swedish, but it would be 40, 60, maybe in that category.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And were those people that weren't one or the other, were they really kind of a separate class -wise?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No, no, other than church -wise. They were separate on the church. But business -wise, friendly -wise, no, you would never know the difference. No, no. And in the bank, there was no discrepancy between Germans. Yeah, you were treated as an individual. But church -wise, they had the Trinity Church here and the Swedes was up there. And when it came time that they consolidated, there was a little resentment, but not too much. But I think the Swedes dominated it. And they were superior. They had the best church. But no, there was no Iraq right now. No, the school was not. I have heard the German community be in a tight -knit one. That reminds me. I have heard the German community at Cameron. Yes. Yes. Now, my parents were German. My mother was German. They could talk German. And of course, during the First World War, my mother and the Second World War was, Hitler was involved. No, it would be the First World War, when the Kaiser was involved. My mother, my mother, and my brother, who were sisters, could talk German. And they used to converse in the phone altogether in German. And all we were considered as anti -American and anti -German and so forth. And it was very bitter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Strong anti -German demons, right?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yes. But Cameron is a German settlement period, so it was pretty much Southwick. But more German than Leland. Cameron and Leland were very much, and they still are German settlers. But American Ridge, that was American.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I don't know. But that's where we all settled. And there was no – there was German, there was English, there was Scotch, there was – there only was the Coxes were the Scots, Binscotters were the English, Eichners were the German, the Brockies were the German. Callison was, I'm sure, English. Not a Swede or Norwegian among them. And that was – our school was that way. And yet you went right down to Kendrick and up the other side, and that was the Dutch, the Germans. And Bear Ridge was – Bear Ridge had a few more Swedes. They had a German – they had a – and they had a – a manual Lutheran, that's the Swede church there. So they were a little bit Swedes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the clan at all, Ku Kluxu?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh, yes. Of course. Yeah, I can remember – yeah, they used to burn the sign. I can remember seeing it burned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: When I lived in Kendrick.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the hillside?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Mm -hmm, yes. But they were not very active. That was right after the First World War. But just enough to stir up a little – I think it was mostly just antagonism. But the IWW was the most – they were the most active. I can remember that. That was after the First World War and during the First World War. Were – I won't work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were farmers – No, that was – Were farmers very worried about them?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No. No, but I think you read more about it than you dealt with them. I think probably Kendrick had a few IWWs. They were agitators. But there was no working people in Kendrick. I think Bovil, though, and Derry had a little run -in with IWWs on the railroads. That's about the only time that you'd see them. But they were more active than the clan.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: You know, good old days. I think one thing I wanted to ask you about was the difference between the healthy and the sick economy. Because you talked to me about this a little bit in the past. That there are things that aren't looking very good now in terms of the economy. And I was just wondering, you said one time to me that if you had the money in the pockets of the working people and the farmers – The laboring people. Yes, I've always contended that if you make the farmer prosperous, you make the laboring – if you have money in the hands of the laboring man, the rest of the world will survive. Because those are the two people that one makes it, the other one spins it. And vice versa. The farmer makes it, he buys, he puts the laboring man to work. And the laboring man works and buys the products of the farmer. And those two people keep the world moving. And you stop and analyze that, study it, compare it, and you'll find that that is true in any agricultural country. Now if you're mining, of course he still has to have the food. If you live in an area like the Arizonas where they have a very limited amount of farming, they still got to buy that food, which makes the farmer prosperous. But you classify the laboring man. He's the buyer. You put money in his pocket and he's going to spend it. They've got to have food. And if he spends it, that takes it out of the farm, off the land. Now that's why unemployment is no good, Sam. See, that destroys that buying power. And that buying power can be so marginal that it can be the profit. It upsets the economy. It upsets the supply and demand.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, I felt that very much after the First Depression. That's what had to be done. Of course, that's what they tried to do, but it was a slow process. You can be three years going into a deep depression. It'll take you 15 to get out of that, to get it right and to get it going. You can't pry in those pumps that fast without destroying that economy, destroying people. It seems to me like the corporations are the ones that are really... Well, corporations, in the 1930s, the corporations were small and there was no... It was easy to handle now. They used to say, well, we went through a depression, but believe me, the government would have never allowed General Motors to go broke. I disagree with that. They could have gone broke. They can't today, though. You let General Motors go broke today. And it's so big. It's as big as government.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: That's what's happened to our country. That's what happened to these big corporations. They control the economy of our country and it's a dangerous, dangerous setup. But I don't know the answer to that one. That is a serious problem that we face. We've allowed that to happen. We have actually allowed these corporations to go on, more or less that they not only own their own company, they own and control hundreds of small corporations besides themselves. That's what the danger part is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What have they done?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: They formed corporations. Now, they should have turned that money over to stockholders. As they accumulated wealth, they've had wealth. Instead of that, they've been allowed to pile it up, buy corporate stocks of other companies. They control the corporations. They control those. They get better deals. They just set up their own companies until they're, instead of making a billion, they're making 2 billion profit. And it's going that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, where's that going to stop, Sam?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: There's nothing to stop it. Now, I had to trust laws. We've had them, but they have not been strict. They've not been conformed to it. And that's one thing that they have never done, is try to stop any corporation from buying another corporation. Buying the stock of another corporation. That's never been.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: You can see that. That's common sense.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you deal with something?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I don't know. There just don't seem to be an answer. Now, a deep, downright terrible depression would do it. But, geez, Sam, think of what it would do. It would ruin the financial. See, we're not so much different between this depression and the last depression. We've got wealth in this depression. There is money. There's money flowing. In the 32 depression, there was no money. There was no way to get any money. Banks were slipping, and they were losing. They had no capital. They had no deposits you couldn't borrow. Now, we don't have that situation yet. We've got, they call it, a recession. I say it has been a depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's a real depression?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: It has to be a depression. Now, there's no way to draw the line. The only thing about a recession, a depression, or a recession only lasts about three months. And you're back on the track again. They were just more or less scared. They just as much as said to this business, now, straighten up, or else you're going to go have a depression. We've been into this thing now, darn near four or five years. That has to be a depression because it started right off the bat, and it never got over it. So, with all this wealth, and this money that's turned loose, in the first depression, there was wealthy people. They had what money there was. There was money, but it was in the hands of the few. They controlled it. That was the General Motors and the Ford Company and the railroads were doing good. The oil companies all had their hands in it. They were all right, but the poor were really poor. And now, today, here you've got the middle class of people, where the husband and the wife is making anywhere from $15 ,000 to $30 ,000 a year. They're spending their money. They're having a good time. They're enjoying themselves. But this guy that's getting $10 ,000 or less, which is unbelievable, plus the unemployed, is having a hell of a time. And there's no relief in sight for him. A lot of them are uneducated. A lot of them can't go up the ladder any farther. They're employed by large banks, by large corporations. Somebody got to die in front of you to get to that, and by that time he's too old. And that's the life that they're leading. And yet, the retirement end of it, the retired people that are on big incomes from retirement and Social Security, they're living pretty good. I could take my Social Security, I could take my retirement, and I could be gone here 30 days out of the month and enjoy myself and travel all the time, and could say I could afford it. I wouldn't enjoy it, but that's how I could live. And yet, there's a depression. And we've got starving people, we've got hungry people, and we've got a stagnation of the economy. And it could get worse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the credit is part of the credit problem?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh my, that's the most abused thing we have. Abusive credit. And you can't blame them here. You can go out now and buy and get yourself a brand new home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The government says we'll suspend the payments, what is it, three years?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: And we give you a cheap interest rate, and that's all you've got to pay. Now, what in the world, that's no answer. See, they talk about the dole, they talk about putting you on relief. That to me is relief. Because that's piling on a debt to somebody under an inflated price, and asking him to pay $35 ,000 for a house, and he pays the interest in ten to three years. If he makes it okay, if he's not, I don't know what we'll do then. That's just exactly what they're saying. Now, I say that I like for people to own their homes, but I don't think they should be giving it to them that easy. I think there should be a principal payment. There should be strings tied to it. And in fact, I think if you buy a $30 ,000 home, you should at least have a couple thousand dollars to pay down on it. Because a lot of these people, Sam, have not learned to save. They've lived from hand to mouth so long, and they live high on the hog. I saw here about two years ago, before this thing was really serious, what impressed me the most, they went into a home. That was when the recession was just starting good. They went into a home to a man that was earning $25 ,000, he and his wife, and they weren't able to make their living and pay their debts. And they used that as an example.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I wanted to say?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I wanted to say that man and that wife needs a manager. Somebody would tell them how to spend their money, and you'd be amazed how many people we got that are doing that.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: You go out here tomorrow, and if you had ten acres of land up here, and it was a very desirable piece of land, and you decided you wanted to sell it, you say, for instance, that ten acres has cost you $5 ,000 five years ago, three years ago. But now I want $30 ,000 for that ten acres. Now somebody's going to pay you that. Now that is inflation, Sam. See, he gathers credit. He goes out and he borrows $30 ,000. He might have $5 ,000, he borrows $25 ,000, and he pays it to me. That is inflation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These guys, these politicians that accuse the Congress for appropriating $5 billion to put unemployment back to work, that's inflation?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No. That's no more than feeding people. If that's inflation, then this whole world is crazy. But the guy that'll come up here and pay that kind of a price for a piece of land that is a fictitious value, that to me is inflation. Every time you walk into the grocery store, if you've got money in your pocket and you're the middle class, and they raise the price of a can of baking powder, 50 cents, and you say, geez, that's quite a raise. Well, I need it. I'll pay it. That's inflation. That starts, those are the wheels that make inflation go. Paying more for a price and putting profit, and that creates volume and volume and volume, and that's what creates inflation. Just like an automobile, they raise them whether they need it or not. General Motors had the biggest profit -taking year in the world. They raised 10 percent. That's inflation. And until we can convince business to quit that and to cut down their profit. But it's in the air. You can raise something, 10 percent, and there's no overproduction. There's money to buy it in the hands of somebody, not the poor, not the unemployed, but the hands of somebody has got that. That's inflation. And we're making that bid, but nobody wants to stop it. It's political dynamite, but price control should have been on this thing at least five years ago. But oh no, it was political suicide. All we got for mixing was wage controls. That's right. And then that never worked. Rent controls, that never worked. Hell, they all come there. No, there was no enforcement. But that's inflation. And it's going to go on and on.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I don't know how you stop it until you do get some kind of controls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the government could possibly be the agent to do that?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yes, yeah. They've granted the president power to stop that right now. But nobody wants to hear that. Now Parker can be threatening him. He said he will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I heard a story about Ollie Bowman. Maybe I even read it in that book by that man. And he once got really mad at a man and said, no man calls me a liar.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember that? Is that a true story?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yeah, it's a true story. Yeah, he was very, very... You call Ollie Bowman a liar or a crook to his face. You better run, no matter who you are. That's true. But this man that he shook and grabbed that way, one was intoxicated. That was over a sail, over a sail of horses. And the other was Ramsdale. But I'd forgotten really what that was about. But he grabbed him by the... He had the counter between you and the guy. He grabbed him by the shirt and was going to pull him right through the window. But that was a liar. But the other one was... He repudiated the bid on a team of horses. That was Walt Johnson's dad. And they had words. And he sat him down in the chair. He was a big man, you know. But he was intoxicated. He made a mistake there. On Ramsdale he did not. Ramsdale, I'm surprised he didn't pull him right through that window and slap him to the ground. Because he could have and he would have. But the other one was a moral mistake because of the fact that the man... He had his side and Ollie had his side. And Ollie was hard of hearing. And it was very, very easy at a sail to make a mistake. He was known to make mistakes. So that one I'd never have pulled him, but on Ramsdale one I did, yes. And I saw him both. But he was... Ollie Bowman was all so very self -respecting when it come to that. He felt he was an honest man. He felt he was a very sober man. And that he was a very honorable man. And boy, you ever told him any different. You had a fight. That was just the way he was built. He took pride in the fact that he was honest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he believe that he could read character pretty well? Character?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yeah, that he could assess. I doubt that. No, I wouldn't say that. No. No, I would just definitely say no to that. Because I don't think he... His mind was never settled that much. That he would study a person. Being religious, and if you're a deep religious man, to me you're a poor judge of humanity. To others you may think you are perfect and you are a symbol to yourself. But to judge others as a rule, those kind of people in my mind often fail. Maybe that's wrong. I don't hold out against Mr. Bowman in any way, shape or form. I would say that he didn't try to do that. I don't think he ever pretended that he could judge other people. I think he was a dishonest man by his experience more than anything else. So he would base his decisions on the major... Mostly acquaintances. And what they were in the community. He and I, I've often thought if they'd have turned either Ollie Bowman or myself loose in a city to run a bank, you wouldn't know where to begin. That's the fact. That's right. I wouldn't even to this day. I would be so scared, I'd be so conservative. Because of that lack of that particular knowledge that you would make a flop before you started. And that's how we were. When we were at home plate on our own ground, nobody could tell us what to do. But if we had had to go to Moscow, even Moscow is not considered a big place. But if we went to Moscow, I think I'd have been lost. That's why I... Any opportunity to go to Moscow now, when the students are so active, I'd be lost. I'm afraid I'd try to help them all. Because a student is a student and you can't do that. There's good and there's bad. But you know, part of your reputation, I think, has been... People have said to me that you have shown a very exceptional ability to evaluate character.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that's true?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: You've got to go back. Now, when we first started making student loans, there was a lot of things I did that people didn't know about. I used to call the mother and the dad of the student who'd come in there and say, I want $300. And I wouldn't know him. In some cases, I knew the name, like an attorney from Orfean or Grainsville. I'd heard of him, see, and they'd come in there and I'd say, well, your parents know about this, no. I would go in the back, I'd say, well, you hang around maybe 15 minutes and I'll see. I'll get some time and I'll see what it looks like. So I would go into my room and I would call 9 times out of 10 and it would probably be the mother because she'd be there in the morning. And I'd say to her, your boy is here, your son is here, he wants $300.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about it? Should he have it or should I say no? She said, what did he want it for?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: And I said, well, he's got some unpaid bills and he owes some book rentals or whatever it happens to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sound legit?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: She'd say, okay, Mr. Rocky, you go ahead. I'll see that you get it. All right. I went back to him, but she said, don't tell him. And of course, he would refrain from me calling his parents and I'd have them out of 10 would. So the whole thing had to be under the board. And that happened many times, yet they didn't know about it. I didn't reveal it. And I wrote it on the application. I'd call his parents. They have okayed this. It was unknown to the student. And so, and I don't, to my knowledge, I don't think there was a parent that repaid it. I did in once or twice have to call the parent and remind, would you tell John that you understand that he was a note at the tribe bank. You'd better pay it. And why next week I'd get the money. See now, those are the things that nobody knew about yet. I got credit for making the loan out of the blue sky off the top of my head. And there was other incidents. You, you did, you sized a kid up. I helped a young gal attorney and I can't think of her name to this day. And she was in real serious help. She needed help. She was on her last year, her last semester up here. And I called and often, I often called the athletic department or the head of the, of the law department and get their records. And I would ask him, I would say now, yeah, I want some help.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, I'd say to them, do you think she's deserving of it?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Absolutely. You'll never have a problem. And we did that time and again to old, well,

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: who was the basketball, you don't know him. Anyway, basketball coaches, we had more pledges from the athletic department because they were always the ones that needed the help. And I would call them, Willis used to call them or whoever made the loan, we'd call and get a verification of their grades and that they're standing and whether they're going to be there and should we loan them $200. And that was also something that was between us and the departments at the university. See there was, and besides the parent, I had that to go with me. And, but I would do it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The chain bank said, oh, the hell would you get out of here?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: We're not calling university. That's their business. But we would, we would call their superior or their professor and get a low down on her. See, which is good. But anyway, this gal, this young attorney, and she had tears in her eyes. And so she needed $600. And that was rough. She couldn't get her, she couldn't graduate because she raised that kind of money. And I let her have it. And by golly, that was one of the most grateful things I think that ever happened. She had a, she had a job. She had a place to go. And she was rated the top in her class. One of the very, very potential good Idaho graduates that went to Washington. And boy, she wasted no time. And that note was paid before the six months was up. And I, that was what I remember. I had nobody to tie to but the university. But her impression to me, her sincerity, the fact she was a girl, I guess maybe it must have helped. A young, a young student, a young girl. She was in her twenties. But I went with her. And boy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it traditionally that bank policies to go real hard on women wanting to be?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No, no, no, no, no. That phase phased out 20 years ago. When I first came to Troy, very seldom would you see a wife come into the bank and even cash a check. The only ones was I told you before is the ailing parent or the widow or somebody that had to carry the ball. But normally your wife, she was afraid to go into a bank. I had, I had the housewives tell me years later, gee, I used to be afraid of you. I would be the last place you were the last guy I ever wanted to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Well, you're the, you were the banker and I was afraid. I was afraid to go in there. But all of a sudden in the years of time, it started changing. And just, you know what you should take for that code.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You believe in Alka-Seltzer?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Should I?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh man, I think that's the greatest. I just got it today. Oh, I think that's great. It was Alka-Seltzer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, so it changed in women's times?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yes. And, and about, oh, probably 1940.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: In the forties. I don't know why. But people, a wife would come in, her husband would be busy. And she's, and she said, we got a note in the mail. We're overdrawn.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there anything I can do? Would you give me a note and I can take it out and have John sign it?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: That's what she'd say. I said, I'll go better than that. If you sign it, Mrs. John Jones, that's all we want. And that started it. You did that and you did it. And you, and you would see people out on the street and you'd say to you as your counselor, well, you're coming to sign a note. The only way it would be good, she'd have to sign it on the back. But it was not considered as a legitimate instrument when the wife signed it. But now it's of course different. But that's how you did it. Once you did it, the word got around why pretty soon they were doing it all. You would just damn lucky to see the husband in six months. They'd be doing all the business. Depositing, borrowing. In fact, I had an instrument, Sam, an incident where it was sad. I had a prominent, and I say prominent, he is a prominent, probably that's employee. And he had his wife and he had a bank account, a joint account. And I wrestled with that woman every damn month. She would be anywhere from four to eight hundred dollars over in Maryland before he'd get his paycheck. And I cried my damnedest to stop her. I'd get her in here. She would cry just like a child. Frank, she says, I can't. And I didn't want to loan her money because that wasn't the answer. She was just simply spending her husband's money before he got it. I said, don't you talk it over with him. I don't dare. He killed me if he knew I was eight hundred dollars over in Maryland.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, well, what are you doing to me? I said, what did he do to me if he doesn't like you for doing it?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: I said, I'm a part of this, too. And she says, oh, my, I never thought of that. And we wrestled with that thing. And finally, it never got any better. And I kept on for another three months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I finally told her, I said, where are you going to have to tell him where I am?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Now you make up your mind. In order to protect myself, somebody has got to talk to him and you've got to find out what's wrong. If he's not making enough or if you're spending it foolishly, he should be in on it. And oh, it was just hard for me to say that. But I knew that the cat has got to get out of the bag these days because he's going to find out. He's going to wonder. And he was making good money. He was making around thousands, twelve hundred dollars a month. And so she promised me, OK, I will. Oh, and it went on. And it took her two months before she finally. And I kept riding her. I sent her over there. I noticed I was not safe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: So finally here they came. He and her together. Oh, man. I thought, boy, the whole damn roof of this institution is going to go right up through the sky. And he apologized. He said he never felt so cheap in his life. He says, I am to blame. I says, to blame you, too. He says, he's far out. I'm the one to blame. I make this money. I've given her the checks. She's deposited them. I never have asked any questions. I've never had a problem. He said, I was setting pretty. I wasn't even worried about the bank. And that went on for years. And finally it came to a head. And brother had loaned him a muck money to get him started equal. And he says from now on, Frank, I'll write the checks. And it changed their whole life around. Here she was losing weight. She was sick. She was a nervous wreck just over it. And you could. And that's understandable. See, I knew that was always happening here. I knew she was going through hell. She just couldn't control herself. No, sir. She was just a poor manager. She had no judgment as to where she got off. And once she got behind, it got impossible to catch up. And the damn paycheck was spent before she got it. And she had to get the groceries. She had to keep the kids in school. She had to clothe them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what would she do but write a check?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: And I couldn't turn it down. And I had two of them just alike that way. They weren't at the same time, but there was two different incidents.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In a lot of families, didn't the woman wind up managing the finance?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yeah, many, many cases the other way around, especially where he played poker or the husband would never bring the check home. Or he would cash it at a tavern on the way home and then bring her maybe two or three hundred dollars. And then say, well, this is all that's left. I spent the rest. And see, now you have those two. But as a rule, that depends on the individual. You can't draw the line and say that that's true in every case. This particular case, both of these cases, the husband was so goddamn tight. That's why I knew that he wasn't going to shed it or turn it away, turn it off just like that. Because, oh, that's why I was figured that God, he's going to be so damn mad and he's going to blame me for allowing it. And of course, when she took the blame, in both cases, she took the blame. She said I'd been wrestling with Frank for the last year. He keeps warning me. He keeps telling me he doesn't know what to do. He's begged me to talk to you and I won't. And so that helped me. They saved my neck and he didn't dare kill her. Even though he might have been mad and no. You know, I wanted to ask you if you'd like to briefly go through some of what happened when you left the bank. And the reason is that I figure historically, even though it's mighty recent, that in the long run it's something that people will probably always wonder about and think about. Especially now that, well, I think they would have anyway, but now the bank has changed hands. Well, we'll make the next session over that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How about it? You want to do it that way?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Yeah, I would rather, yeah. Okay, I wasn't really sure if it'd be worth, you know, wanting to go through a big hit. It wouldn't be long and I've learned to be reasonable. I've got the hate and I've got the disgust for him, but that don't change anything. And I don't have to be that way and still tell exactly what's happened. No, I wouldn't object to that. In fact, I would, so fellas, it'd probably be better to get it off your chest because there's nothing unusual about it. There's no secrets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the way it turned out, why?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Farmers and loggers between the two types of people you seem as much as you. Well, I think you should be one or the other, however. And it didn't used to be. In other words, in the olden days, if you logged yourself or your neighbor, you were a farmer and a logger because the average farmer, his background was logging. He came here and he cleared his land. He worked in the sawmill and farmed his ten or forty acres. And they worked side by side. And eventually, as he got more length, he finally got enough that he could make his living. He made it off of that. He turned to farming from logging. So sure, there's a connection. But the younger generation, though, that went into logging have stuck by it. They've been well paid. It's a dangerous profession, though, because I know there's been many a housewife that has bid her husband goodbye in the morning at five o 'clock and knows in the back of her mind he might not get home tonight. And it was that treacherous for years and years. It never failed. Every summer we lost a good young man due to a logging accident, sometimes two and three a year. But now, of course, it's a little bit the thing of the past. They've become more conscious. The chainsaw has made them bunch up. In other words, you don't go here and you don't go here and saw it on a tree now. They stick together and they go in like they're clans. And that's helped. The type of people, it seemed to me that the farmers were meaning somewhat different kind of life. Although maybe I sometimes feel they were quite similar in the families, but then sometimes I think they were rather different in the kind of life. Well, of course, we have farmers that are now 50 and 60 and 70 years old that never logged in their life. They weren't loggers and yet you go out here and pick out many, many of them. And that's what they did when they first came to Troy. They were loggers and they eventually went into the farm. It was easier. And if you got enough land fared, of course, then you just became a farmer and the heck with the logging. Now, I know of lots of young people today whose fathers are loggers that don't encourage their kids to go to the woods at all because of the hazard and because of the danger of it. But they're well paid. Man, I don't know. You know, you compare a logger to a schoolteacher, the logger gets the most money. And yet he has no education from the standpoint that he's required to go to college to be a logger.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in the early days there was much difference in so far as, like, say, drinking in the farm?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Oh, yeah. Yeah, you took that for granted. Yes, I'd say probably 60 percent of them drank. Yes. Usually on weekends, very seldom would you see a logger that would be sober Monday morning enough to get on the job. And by Friday he was darn thirsty. So he wasn't drinking. But I also have known loggers that never quit that had the bottle with them consistently and they're sad. But they seem to be nippers rather than heavy drinkers. They would just nip at that bottle all day long and get a good meal and sleep in a cold bed. And they got by. But they suffered for it in the end, of course. And farm families and men would tend to drink much better. No, no. No, I think that's up to the community. I've been told, I don't know how true it is, that there's quite a bit of drinking on the prairie. But how true it is, I don't know. I wouldn't say that. But normally, no, a farmer, if he comes to town, he gets a glass of beer. When he would drink that beer and go home, and that's the day they don't, I don't think there's a rule with farmers. Very few of them are steady drinkers. But then there's a difference that struck me too. The payroll that the loggers on are making this money, you know, piece by piece. And the farmer would have won. Yeah, he's a one -shot deal. Yeah. And of course, there's good and there's bad to that. A lot of them, or rather, get on the monthly payroll. At least they got a guaranteed income. And they know how much they're going to get. The farmer gambles on the weather, and he gambles on the price, and he gambles on those elements that he has to fight to see that he saves his crop. But I suppose even the logger gets laid off at least anywhere from one to three months out of the year too. And he doesn't arrange his affairs, but he has a heck of a time to undo the layoffs. He doesn't have any money either. So it works both ways. That's his fault. I've known young people who worked in the woods when I first came to Troy. Or say during the 30s, they were young people. And until 1960 or 1965, never had nothing. And all of a sudden, I don't know what did it, what caused them to change, but they started saving money. And before they retired, they had a bank account of $5 ,000 to $10 ,000 in savings. Now that's unbelievable. But that's true.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were these loggers?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: These were logging men. They weren't, when you say logger, while they were sawyers, they were that type of person.

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: How is the historical society money-wise?

speaker: Frank or Margie Brocke
words: Are they hard up?
